items.question: State the Ridge and Lasso objective functions.
items.answer: Ridge minimizes ||y - Xβ||₂² + λ||β||₂². Lasso minimizes ||y - Xβ||₂² + λ||β||₁. Both are penalized least squares; only the penalty shape differs (L2 vs L1).

items.question: Why does Ridge have a closed-form solution but Lasso usually does not?
items.answer: Ridge's L2 penalty keeps the objective strictly convex quadratic; setting the gradient to zero yields (XᵀX + λI)β = Xᵀy. Lasso's |βⱼ| terms are non-differentiable at zero, so solutions often require iterative methods (coordinate descent, proximal gradient).

items.question: When would you choose Lasso over Ridge?
items.answer: When you need a sparse interpretable model—many weak features and you want automatic subset selection (p ≫ n, genomics, text counts). Ridge is preferable when predictors are correlated and you want all variables retained with shared shrinkage.

items.question: How does Ridge relate to a Bayesian prior?
items.answer: Ridge is MAP estimation for linear regression with Gaussian noise and independent Gaussian priors βⱼ ~ N(0, τ²). The L2 penalty is the negative log-prior; λ ∝ 1/τ²—tighter priors mean larger λ and more shrinkage toward zero.
